_patch_meta.session_ref: M10b Phase 2 Pass A meaning record via Claude API
_patch_meta.source: Claude API meaning record (Sonnet 4.6); 514 verses across 17 terms

set.analysis_note: The wicked are those whose moral status is the opposite of the righteous — Abraham's question implies that wickedness and righteousness are distinct inner conditions that ought to receive distinct judgments.
The wicked are those who deserve death as a just consequence of their condition — conflating them with the righteous would violate the divine judge's moral character.
The man in the wrong is the aggressor in the fight — his wickedness is an active behavioral choice to strike a companion, a relational violation observed and named by an outsider.
Pharaoh acknowledges being in the wrong in contrast to God's rightness — this is a moment of conscience surfacing under pressure, even if the admission is situational rather than transformative.
The wicked man recruits others into complicity through false testimony — wickedness here is a social and moral force that corrupts the will of bystanders drawn into alliance with it.
The wicked are those God will not acquit — their guilt is objective and permanent before divine justice, standing in contrast to the innocent whom must be protected.
A specific sexual act is declared an abomination — the term names a category-violation that evokes divine revulsion, marking the act as fundamentally disordered against the created order.
The abominations are a defined set of practices that the community — native and stranger alike — must entirely abstain from, making avoidance of them an act of obedience to divine statutes.

The prior inhabitants committed all these abominations and thereby defiled the land itself — the acts carry a polluting power that extends beyond the individual to the moral state of the created environment.
Performing any of these abominable acts results in being cut off from the community — the penalty names the social and covenantal consequence of acts that violate the divine order.
These abominable customs are practices that make the self unclean — keeping the divine charge means refusing to let these patterns from the past take hold in one's own behavior.
Both parties in the act are said to have committed an abomination — guilt is mutual and the act itself is the abomination, carrying a death penalty that signals its gravity before God.
The wicked men's sins have a contagious power — proximity to them risks being swept away, so the community must actively distance itself to preserve moral and physical safety.
Idols of foreign gods are an abomination to God — coveting what is on them ensnares the will, drawing a person into what God utterly detests.
Bringing an abominable thing into one's household transfers its devoted-to-destruction status — the abomination has a contaminating power that re-orders the will of anyone who tolerates it.

The abominable things the nations do for their gods — including child sacrifice — are what God hates; their worship is an inner and ritual corruption that distorts the will toward what is most detestable.
The abomination here is an act of communal apostasy requiring diligent investigation before judgment — its gravity demands certainty before the community responds.
Offering a flawed animal is an abomination — the inner act of presenting something defective to God reveals a disordered valuation of God's worth, treating worship carelessly.
An abomination done in the community requires diligent inquiry before punishment — the term signals an act so serious that its truth must be verified before the community acts.
The nations' practices are abominable — Israel is warned not to learn or imitate them, meaning the will must actively resist adopting patterns God detests.
Whoever practices divination and occult arts becomes personally an abomination to God — the repeated acts are the reason God is expelling the nations, making the practitioner repellent to the divine.
Idolatrous practices are abominable and contagious — allowing the nations to teach them risks corrupting Israel's will to sin against God.
Cross-dressing is declared an abomination — the act violates a divinely ordered distinction between male and female, making the one who does it repellent to God.
Bringing a prostitute's earnings or a male prostitute's fee into God's house is an abomination — it profanes the sacred space by mixing what is detestable with what is holy.
Remarrying a defiled former wife is an abomination before God — the act would sin against the moral-covenantal order and bring guilt upon the land.
set.keywords: ["defilement relational", "moral order violated", "land polluted", "covenant abomination", "act prohibited"]

set.analysis_note: In a legal dispute the judge is to condemn the guilty party — the wickedness is a legally and morally adjudicated status that must be named and punished to uphold justice.
The guilty man's wickedness is measured and proportionate punishment is assigned — the inner moral status translates directly into a just and bounded legal consequence.
Dishonest commercial practice — false weights and measures — makes a person an abomination to God, linking moral character directly to the quality of one's daily transactions.
A secretly made and set-up idol is an abomination to God — the secrecy reveals that the heart knows the act is wrong even while the will proceeds with it.
The evil of deeds is the moral quality of actions that flows from forsaking God — the corrupt inner disposition toward God produces a corrupted will that acts against him, bringing divine judgment.
The detestable idols of surrounding nations are objects that Israel has directly witnessed — the memory of seeing them is a warning against internalizing the reverence others gave them.
Worshipping abominations provokes God to anger and jealousy — the inner dynamic is a relational betrayal where the people's will turns to what is detestable, injuring the divine-human bond.
The wicked are those who will be silenced in darkness — their wickedness is an inner condition that ultimately cannot sustain itself, cut off by God's sovereign power.
Presumption and self-assertion — here called iniquity — are treated as equivalent to idolatry; the inner trouble is a will that elevates its own judgment above the divine word.
set.keywords: ["presumption inward", "will self asserting", "idolatry equated", "word rejected", "heart exalting"]

set.analysis_note: Eliab accuses David of evil in the heart — projecting an inner disposition of pride and presumption onto his motives, locating the evil specifically in the heart as its seat.
The proverb asserts that wickedness is the natural and inevitable fruit of a wicked inner source — the wicked person is constitutionally oriented toward producing wicked action.
Wicked men who kill a righteous man bear a blood-guilt that demands divine retribution — wickedness here is an act of moral violence that places its perpetrators under irrevocable judgment.
God is asked to condemn the guilty by bringing their own conduct back on their heads — wickedness here is morally self-incriminating behavior that divine justice returns upon the doer.
set.keywords: ["guilt self incriminating", "conduct returned", "divine judgment", "justice retributive", "wickedness behavioral"]

set.analysis_note: The foreign deity Milcom is labeled an abomination — the term names the detestable character of a false god that draws the heart away from covenant loyalty.
set.keywords: ["idol detestable", "heart drawn away", "loyalty betrayed", "false deity", "divine revulsion"]

set.analysis_note: Two foreign gods are explicitly named abominations — their shrines are built by a king whose will has been corrupted through relational compromise, institutionalizing the detestable.
Cultic prostitution represents a full adoption of the abominations of expelled nations — the community's will has conformed entirely to practices God declared detestable.
Child sacrifice is named as a detestable practice of expelled nations — a king's will has followed the moral pattern of those nations rather than the covenant order, enacting what God most despises.
Doing evil according to the detestable practices of expelled nations is a wholesale moral conformity — the king's will is oriented by foreign abominations rather than the divine standard.

Manasseh's abominations exceed those of the Amorites and actively recruit others into sin — the evil is escalating, communal, and implicating, reflecting a will given entirely over to detestable acts.
The foreign deities are named individually as abominations — their high places are defiled by the reforming king, repudiating what a previous king's corrupted will had institutionalized.
set.keywords: ["idol detestable", "apostasy reversed", "will corrupted former", "defilement removed", "reform enacted"]

set.analysis_note: The same idols are labeled abominations (to.e.vah) — the reforming king's act of defiling their shrines is a repudiation of the detestable practices that had long corrupted worship.
Josiah purges all the abominable objects and practices visible in the land — the reform is a comprehensive act of will to uproot every detestable thing in order to re-establish covenant obedience.
God is asked to repay the guilty by returning their own conduct on their heads — the wicked person's actions are themselves the measure and instrument of just retribution.
set.keywords: ["guilt retributive", "conduct returned", "divine justice", "wickedness self incriminating", "judgment enacted"]

set.analysis_note: Detestable idols are physical objects that must be actively removed from the land — the king's reform is an act of will prompted by prophetic courage to purge what is abhorrent.
Helping the wicked and loving those who hate God are treated as equivalent relational failures that bring divine wrath — aligning one's will with the wicked corrupts one's standing before God.
set.keywords: ["alliance evil", "will aligning", "divine wrath provoked", "loyalty misplaced", "relational corruption"]

set.analysis_note: A woman whose inner character is defined as wicked had channeled that disposition into desecrating sacred space, raiding holy things for idolatrous use — wickedness expressed as religious violation.
The king enacted the abominable practices of surrounding nations — child sacrifice — showing a will that deliberately conformed to what God had expelled as morally intolerable.
set.keywords: ["abomination enacted", "will conforming", "conscience overridden", "imitation corrupt", "moral boundary crossed"]

set.analysis_note: He replicated the inner orientation of expelled nations — doing evil before God — revealing a will aligned with abominable practices rather than divine standard.
set.keywords: ["will aligned evil", "abomination replicated", "conscience absent", "orientation corrupt", "defection deliberate"]

set.analysis_note: Abominations are here treated as removable external objects, and their removal enables a whole people to maintain devotion — the inner corruption had been externally embodied in objects that could be stripped away.
Jehoiakim's abominations are recorded acts done by him — willful deeds of moral outrage that the official record preserves as defining his reign.
Leaders and people together chose to follow abominable practices of other nations, showing collective unfaithfulness that actively desecrated what had been set apart as holy.
set.keywords: ["abomination followed", "unfaithfulness collective", "will choosing corrupt", "holiness violated", "conscience collective silenced"]

set.analysis_note: Failure to maintain separation from peoples defined by abominable practices reveals a will that chose entanglement over distinctness, merging inner identity with what was morally contaminating.
The speaker fears that repeated intermarriage with abomination-practicing peoples signals a will that cannot stop transgressing, inviting consuming divine anger.
set.keywords: ["defection repeated", "will recurring", "abomination practiced", "divine anger invited", "conscience failing"]

set.analysis_note: The term here names inner sadness of heart visible on the face — not moral evil but inward distress; this verse does not directly evidence wickedness-cluster inner content for the term ro.a.
The wicked are characterised by their relentless troubling of others — a perpetual, agitating inner drive that only death can stop.
Those who deliberately cultivate iniquity and trouble as a practice reap corresponding ruin — the inner posture of plowing evil is treated as a chosen, effortful disposition.
set.keywords: ["iniquity cultivated", "will choosing harm", "trouble sown", "consequence matching", "effort corrupt"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked are people whose inner stance of hostility will result in shame-covering and annihilation — their wickedness defines an existence destined for erasure.
The speaker observes that blameless and wicked alike suffer destruction, questioning whether inner moral character actually determines fate — a crisis of moral intelligibility.
set.keywords: ["moral distinction questioned", "conscience crisis", "justice doubted", "suffering indiscriminate", "will perplexed"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked are portrayed as those into whose hands the earth is delivered — their inner character of domination and injustice shapes the very structure of public reality.
The wicked have designs — inner schemes — that God is accused of favouring over the righteous; wickedness here is a purposeful inner plotting.
God perceives and registers worthless iniquity in a person — the inner state of moral emptiness is fully visible to divine scrutiny even when hidden from others.
set.keywords: ["iniquity perceived", "divine seeing", "moral emptiness", "inner state exposed", "scrutiny divine"]

set.analysis_note: Iniquity is located 'in the hand' — an active inner hold that must be deliberately released and distanced, and injustice must be expelled from the dwelling.
The wicked face total inner and external collapse — failing sight, no escape, and a hope reduced to death — their wickedness producing a terminal inner desolation.
The wicked man's inner state is one of constant writhing pain throughout his entire life — wickedness is linked to an unrelenting inner torment that spans all his days.
Trouble and evil are portrayed as conceived and birthed from within — the inner being is a womb that generates deceit, making iniquity a generative inner process.
set.keywords: ["evil conceived", "inner generating", "deceit birthed", "will producing harm", "iniquity gestating"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked are agents into whose hands the sufferer is delivered — their wickedness is an active, harmful power directed against another person.
The wicked person's inner vitality — symbolised as light and flame — is extinguished; wickedness carries within itself the seed of its own darkening.
set.keywords: ["inner light extinguished", "vitality failing", "wickedness self-consuming", "darkness inner", "spirit quenched"]

set.analysis_note: The exulting joy of the wicked is characterised as momentary — their inner elation is real but radically brief, underlining the instability of a wicked inner disposition.
set.keywords: ["joy transient", "exulting brief", "inner elation unstable", "wickedness impermanent", "delight collapsing"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked man's portion and heritage are divinely decreed — his inner character of wickedness determines a fixed destiny of judgment assigned by God.

The speaker challenges the assumed link between wickedness and suffering — the wicked apparently thrive and grow powerful, raising the question of whether inner evil actually produces its due consequence.
set.keywords: ["wicked prospering", "justice questioned", "consequence absent", "inner evil unpenalised", "moral order doubted"]

set.analysis_note: The counsel — inner deliberative wisdom — of the wicked is something the speaker explicitly distances himself from, showing that wickedness generates a morally alien mode of reasoning.
The speaker questions how often the wicked truly suffer their lamp being extinguished — inner wickedness seems to avoid the calamity that justice would demand.
set.keywords: ["punishment questioned", "wicked unscathed", "justice deferred", "inner evil unpunished", "lamp enduring"]

set.analysis_note: Iniquity is described as stored up and deferred — an inner moral debt that accumulates and is eventually paid, but the timing is disputed as too delayed to serve as warning.
The interlocutors point to the ruined tent of the wicked as expected evidence of divine judgment — but the speaker questions whether such ruins actually mark wicked persons.
set.keywords: ["ruin expected", "wickedness doubted", "judgment questioned", "evidence challenged", "moral assumption tested"]

set.analysis_note: The 'old way' trodden by wicked men is a habitual path of iniquity — a worn moral trajectory that others are warned not to follow, implying that wickedness has a persistent, repeating character.
Despite material blessing, the counsel of the wicked — their inner deliberative frame — is something the speaker emphatically rejects as morally alien to himself.
set.keywords: ["counsel rejected", "inner reasoning alien", "will disowning", "moral boundary", "wickedness repudiated"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked man is here defined by his possession of land and vineyard whose produce the destitute must glean — his wickedness manifests as exploitative ownership over others.
set.keywords: ["exploitation active", "possession corrupt", "power over others", "wickedness structural", "deprivation caused"]

set.analysis_note: The speaker invokes wickedness as the worst imaginable identity for an enemy — to be wicked is the inner state equivalent to being utterly condemned.
set.keywords: ["wickedness condemned", "identity worst", "enmity named", "moral curse", "inner state lowest"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked man's portion is defined in relation to God and the Almighty — his inner character earns a divinely assigned heritage of suffering and loss.
Calamity and disaster are identified as the natural consequence reserved for the unrighteous and workers of iniquity — their inner laboring in evil generates a matching external ruin.
set.keywords: ["iniquity labored", "consequence matching", "ruin deserved", "unrighteous punished", "cause effect moral"]

set.analysis_note: Choosing to travel and associate with evildoers and wicked men reveals a will that deliberately affiliates with iniquity — moral character shaped by chosen companions.
Walking with wicked men reflects a chosen inner alignment — the person's will has opted for the company of wickedness, shaping their own moral trajectory.
God is declared utterly incapable of wickedness or wrongdoing — divine nature is the absolute structural opposite of the inner disposition that wickedness names.
set.keywords: ["wickedness impossible", "divine nature opposed", "moral purity absolute", "wrong excluded", "character divine"]

set.analysis_note: The term 'wicked' is used as a label applied by divine authority to nobles — God's perspective strips pretension away and names the inner moral reality of persons regardless of social rank.
set.keywords: ["wickedness named", "divine judgment", "inner reality exposed", "rank irrelevant", "moral verdict"]

set.analysis_note: Evildoers cannot hide in any darkness — their inner moral condition is fully transparent to divine perception, with no concealment possible.
set.keywords: ["hiding impossible", "inner state exposed", "divine perception", "concealment failed", "evildoer seen"]

set.analysis_note: God publicly strikes the wicked for their wickedness — the inner character of wickedness draws visible, public divine retribution, making the moral verdict transparent to all.
set.keywords: ["wickedness punished", "retribution public", "divine striking", "inner evil exposed", "judgment visible"]

set.analysis_note: The speaker is accused of answering like wicked men — his words align him with the inner disposition of evildoers, implicating speech as a mirror of inner moral character.
set.keywords: ["speech revealing", "inner character mirrored", "wicked likened", "words aligning", "moral verdict speech"]

set.analysis_note: Wickedness is declared to concern only the person himself — it damages the human sphere, not God, locating the inner harm of wickedness within human relational and moral space.
set.keywords: ["wickedness self-affecting", "harm human", "inner damage limited", "conscience human", "moral harm bounded"]

set.analysis_note: God does not preserve the wicked alive — their inner character of wickedness forfeits life itself, while the afflicted receive justice, showing wickedness as life-threatening from within.
set.keywords: ["life forfeited", "wickedness fatal", "divine withholding", "justice contrasted", "inner character deadly"]

set.analysis_note: God opens the ears as an act of instruction directed specifically at returning from iniquity — iniquity is a direction the will has taken, and the inner ear must be opened to reverse it.
set.keywords: ["iniquity as direction", "will returning", "hearing opened", "instruction divine", "repentance enabled"]

set.analysis_note: The judgment reserved for the wicked has seized the speaker himself — the inner experience of divine judgment bears down on him, implicating his own situation in the fate of the wicked.
set.keywords: ["judgment seizing", "wicked fate personal", "conscience gripped", "inner pressure divine", "verdict applied"]

set.analysis_note: Iniquity is a turn that the will actively chooses over affliction — the inner faculty of choice is warned not to pivot toward iniquity, showing it as a deliberate volitional act.
Dawn shakes the wicked out of the earth like vermin from a garment — their inner character makes them incompatible with creation's ordered renewal; they must be expelled.
set.keywords: ["wicked expelled", "creation opposing", "inner incompatibility", "disorder removed", "moral order restored"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked are denied light and their uplifted arm — symbol of violent agency — is broken; their inner drive toward harm is stripped of its power by divine action.
set.keywords: ["light withheld", "arm broken", "violent agency stopped", "inner drive frustrated", "power denied"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked are those who stand in pride and must be trodden down — wickedness here is fused with pride as an inner stance of self-elevation that demands divine humiliation.
The blessed man refuses to align himself with those whose inner orientation is wicked — he avoids their counsel, their paths, and their company entirely.
The wicked lack inner rootedness and substance; they are as insubstantial as chaff — easily swept away because they have no stable moral or spiritual foundation.
The wicked cannot stand before divine judgment; their inner character disqualifies them from belonging to the community of the righteous.
The way of the wicked is unknown and unguarded by God and therefore ends in destruction, contrasting the inner relational security of the righteous.
The wicked are portrayed as having aggressive, biting power that God dismantles — their destructive capacity is broken by divine intervention.
Those who do evil cannot stand before God because God actively hates them — their inner troublemaking is incompatible with his holy presence.
Workers of evil are commanded to depart because their troublemaking is utterly incompatible with the grieving, weeping soul that God hears and receives.
The wicked have an active evil that must be terminated; God tests minds and hearts precisely to distinguish the wicked from the righteous at the deepest inner level.
Evil is portrayed as an internal generative process — conceived, gestated, and birthed from within the wicked person, producing lies and mischief as its offspring.
God's judgment erases the wicked entirely — their name, memory, and existence are blotted out, indicating the absolute moral illegitimacy of their inner nature.
The wicked are ensnared by the very deeds their own hands devised — their inner schemes turn against them as the mechanism of divine justice.
set.keywords: ["self entrapment", "scheme backfiring", "will self defeating", "judgment enacted", "deed recoiling"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked are defined here by their forgetting of God — a deep inner orientation away from God that leads to their return to Sheol.
Arrogance drives the wicked to actively pursue and oppress the vulnerable — pride is the inner engine powering their oppressive behavior.
The wicked person boasts openly about the desires of his own soul and renounces God — his inner appetites are glorified while God is despised.
set.keywords: ["soul desires boasted", "appetite glorified", "god renounced", "will self-serving", "conscience silenced"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked person's pride so fills his inner vision that he does not seek God; his every thought is shaped by practical atheism — God is excluded from his mind.
Iniquity resides beneath the tongue — a hidden inner reservoir of trouble and harm that floods outward through words of cursing, deceit, and oppression.
The wicked renounce God from within their heart, convinced there is no accountability — the inner denial of divine judgment fuels their defiance.
A prayer that God would break the power of the wicked and call their wickedness to full account — implying that wickedness has a measurable, exhaustible record.
The wicked covertly aim their weapons at the upright in heart — their inner hostility targets those whose inward integrity stands in contrast to them.
God's soul actively hates the wicked — wickedness here is the object of divine loathing, especially paired with the love of violence as the inner disposition.
set.keywords: ["divine hatred", "violence loved", "soul orientation evil", "abhorrence aroused", "inner disposition hostile"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked receive destruction as their appointed portion — fire, sulfur, and scorching wind are the just outcome of their inner character and deeds.
The wicked are emboldened to prowl when vileness is culturally exalted — their inner character expands to fill a social environment that honors baseness.
set.keywords: ["vileness exalted", "boldness wicked", "social corruption", "evil emboldened", "moral environment degraded"]

set.analysis_note: Evildoers lack moral knowledge — they exploit and consume others without awareness or care, and their inner orientation excludes calling on God.
set.keywords: ["knowledge absent", "evil consuming", "awareness moral missing", "god uncalled", "exploitation habitual"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked are characterized by active violence directed against the psalmist — their inner hostility has deadly intent and relentless pursuit.
The soul needs deliverance from the wicked — their threat is so profound it endangers the soul itself, requiring divine intervention to subdue them.
The psalmist expresses deliberate moral hatred for evildoers and refuses association with the wicked — an act of will maintaining inner integrity.
These evildoers carry inner evil while outwardly speaking peace — a radical split between inner evil and outward speech reveals deep inner duplicity.
The wicked are those who speak peace to neighbors while harboring evil in their hearts — their wickedness is an inner reality masked by social performance.
The evil of deeds is a measurable moral quality that deserves exact retribution — the inner character of harmful works has a just recompense.
The wicked deserve shame and silence before God — in contrast to the psalmist who calls upon God, the wicked are destined for shameful, voiceless descent.
The wicked accumulate many sorrows as the inner consequence of their orientation — their inner state produces suffering, unlike the love that surrounds the trusting.
Affliction will slay the wicked — their inner enmity toward the righteous brings about their own condemnation, as hatred rebounds on the hater.
set.keywords: ["hatred rebounding", "condemnation certain", "affliction slaying", "enmity self destroying", "judgment inevitable"]

set.analysis_note: Transgression speaks internally to the wicked person as a voice deep in his heart, while the fear of God is entirely absent — wickedness is an inner voice that displaces divine fear.
set.keywords: ["transgression speaking", "heart corrupted", "fear absent", "inner voice evil", "god displaced"]

set.analysis_note: Evil trouble flows out through the mouth in words of deceit — the inner corruption has displaced wisdom, and the will to do good has ceased.
Trouble is plotted in the quiet of the bed — evil is cultivated deliberately in the inner life during rest, and the will refuses to reject it.
set.keywords: ["evil plotted", "will refusing rejection", "planning deliberate", "inner cultivation", "night scheming"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked are a source of forceful displacement — their inner arrogance extends as a hand that drives away the vulnerable and righteous.
Evildoers end in total collapse — they are thrust down and cannot rise, showing that the trajectory of inner evil terminates in irrecoverable ruin.
The wicked will soon be gone without a trace — their seeming permanence is illusory; their inner wickedness has no lasting ground to stand on.
The wicked person plots and gnashes teeth against the righteous — active inner hostility expressed through planning and visceral anger directed at the upright.
The wicked arm themselves with the intent to bring down the poor and upright — their inner disposition turns capability into premeditated destructive action.
The abundance of the wicked is morally hollow compared to the righteous person's little — inner righteousness makes modest resources more valuable than wicked wealth.
set.keywords: ["abundance hollow", "moral quality wealth", "comparison inner", "righteousness surpassing", "wickedness diminished"]

set.analysis_note: The strength and power of the wicked will be broken — their capacity for harm depends on an arm that God will shatter, ending their oppressive force.
set.keywords: ["power broken", "strength destroyed", "capacity evil removed", "divine opposition", "wicked disarmed"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked will perish and vanish like smoke — their apparent glory is momentary, revealing that their inner orientation leads only to dissolution.
The wicked borrow and do not repay — a moral failure of will and character that contrasts with the generous inner disposition of the righteous.
The visitor speaks empty words outwardly while inwardly the heart is gathering iniquity — a profound inner-outer split where the heart secretly collects evil.
Those who work evil lack moral knowledge — their exploitation of others and failure to call on God reveals an inner blindness that enables predatory behavior.
The enemy drops trouble upon the psalmist with anger and sustained grudge — inner malice is held persistently and expressed as oppressive, crushing pressure.
Iniquity and trouble are structural features of the city itself — evil is not merely personal but embedded as a pervasive inner reality within communal life.
set.keywords: ["iniquity pervasive", "trouble embedded", "communal corruption", "evil structural", "environment moral corrupt"]

set.analysis_note: The psalmist appeals for justice against the crime of enemies — their wrongdoing carries an inner culpability that demands divine punishment.
Workers of evil are paired with bloodthirsty men — their inner disposition is one of harm-working that requires divine rescue to escape.
Evil here is the treacherous inner plotting against others — a deliberate, scheming disposition of the will that seeks to harm, prompting a call for divine judgment.
Evil is located in the hidden, secretive scheming of those who devise harm — an inner conspiring that operates covertly and in collective solidarity.
Evil here is iniquity that is cherished and harbored in the heart — an inner holding onto wrongdoing that closes off divine communication and breaks relationship with God.
Evildoers flourish outwardly like grass, but their inner character is oriented toward destruction — the verse frames their apparent prosperity as temporary and their ultimate end as ruin.
Evildoers are identified as enemies of God whose inner opposition to him results in their scattering and perishing — their evil is defined by its direction against the divine.
Evildoers express their inner character through arrogant boasting — the overflow of proud, unrestrained speech reveals a will and tongue oriented toward self-exaltation rather than righteousness.
The verse highlights the power and boldness of evildoers by asking who will stand against them — their evil is a force that oppresses and requires a courageous advocate to oppose.
The evil embedded in evildoers is turned back upon them by God — their iniquity is their own, accumulated and returned as judgment, showing that wickedness is self-implicating.
Evildoers are those whose inner character disqualifies them from the community of the Lord — their removal is an act of moral purification, cutting off those whose lives contradict divine order.
Iniquity is here a power that seeks to gain dominion over the inner person — the prayer asks for steadiness so that evil does not seize control of the will and steer one's steps.
Those who turn aside to crooked ways are classified as evildoers whose inner deviation from righteousness leads them to share in the fate of those who persistently do wrong.
Iniquity is something the heart can incline toward and be drawn into through association — the verse prays against the heart's susceptibility to be pulled into evil deeds by inner inclination.
Evildoers are characterized by laying traps and snares — their evil is expressed in cunning, predatory intent directed against the innocent, requiring protection from outside the self.
The wicked are defined by a character that is treacherous and devious, and their wickedness results in uprooting — they have no lasting place because their inner orientation is against righteousness.
The ruin of the wicked is presented as an external threat that need not be feared — wickedness leads to catastrophic collapse, and the righteous are implicitly protected from sharing that fate.
The devious person — one whose inner way is crooked and twisted — is an abomination to God, meaning their inner character itself is the source of divine rejection and exclusion from intimacy.
The wicked person's inner character brings a curse upon their household — their wickedness radiates outward, contaminating their dwelling, while the righteous receive blessing.
The path of the wicked is a way of life one can choose to enter or avoid — wickedness is here a directional orientation of the will that leads down a dangerous road requiring active avoidance.
The way of the wicked is characterized by moral darkness — they lack inner perception and stumble without knowing why, revealing that wickedness blinds the inner faculty of moral sight.
The wicked person is entrapped by his own iniquities — wickedness is self-binding, creating cords that hold the person fast, showing that evil is an inner bondage generated by one's own acts.
The worthless, wicked person is identified by crooked speech — their inner character expresses itself in deceptive, corrupt verbal output, revealing a distorted inner orientation.
Seven things are abominations to God — the category itself frames certain inner attitudes and actions as fundamentally repugnant to the divine, though specific content follows in subsequent verses.
Evil here is located in the heart that actively devises wicked plans — it is an inner originating faculty that generates schemes, paired with feet that eagerly execute them.
Wickedness is an abomination to Wisdom's lips — the inner voice of wisdom recoils from and refuses to utter evil, defining wickedness as that which truth and integrity cannot tolerate.
The wicked person responds to correction with injury and abuse — wickedness is here shown in the defensive, aggressive inner reaction against moral correction, resisting reproof.
The craving of the wicked — their inner desire — is frustrated and thwarted by God, showing that wickedness produces destructive appetites that are ultimately denied their object.
The mouth of the wicked conceals violence — their speech hides inner violent intent, revealing a duplicity in which outward words mask an inward disposition toward harm.
The name of the wicked will rot — their legacy decays because their inner character was corrupt; wickedness leaves no worthy memory, contrasting with the blessing of the righteous.
The mouth of the wicked conceals violence — as in Pro 10:6, the inner violent orientation is hidden behind speech, showing that wickedness operates through concealment and misdirection.
The gain of the wicked leads to sin — their inner orientation converts even earnings into further wrongdoing, showing that wickedness has a self-perpetuating, sin-generating momentum.
The heart of the wicked is of little worth — wickedness is located at the center of inner value and worth; the wicked person's innermost self is deficient and without moral substance.

set.keywords: ["abomination adopted", "righteousness forfeited", "will defecting", "inner turning fatal", "treachery enacted"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked person's inner orientation can reverse: turning away from committed wickedness and choosing just action saves life, showing wickedness is not fixed but volitional.
The abominations of the fathers are a body of practiced evil deserving judicial confrontation — the inner legacy of corrupt worship that must be named and exposed.
The detestable things are described as objects the eyes feast on, locating abomination in visual desire and inner attachment to idols that defiles the person.
Continued feasting of the eyes on detestable things, paired with refusal to listen, shows abomination rooted in hardened will and defiant inner attachment.
The people replicate their fathers' inner pattern of spiritual adultery by going after detestable things, showing abomination as a habitual, inherited inner defection.
Wickedness is referenced only as a category distinguishing one class of persons; the verse does not develop its inner-being content but uses it as a moral descriptor in a judgment declaration.
Wickedness again functions as a moral category; the judgment sweeps both righteous and wicked alike, underscoring the totality of divine response without elaborating inner content.
The prince is addressed as profanely wicked — a deep inner-character designation coupling ritual defilement with moral corruption — as his day of final punishment arrives.
The profane wicked are those sustained by false visions and divined lies, whose inner state of wickedness brings them to the moment of final punishment.
The city's abominations are accumulated inner-and-outer corruptions requiring full judicial declaration — the sum of evil practices that mark it as a bloody city.
Abomination is expressed through sexual violation of the neighbor's wife, daughter-in-law, and sister — concrete acts of boundary-breaking desire that defile relational and moral order.
The two women's abominations are a pattern requiring judicial declaration, indicating accumulated moral corruption across a lifetime of defection demanding naming.
The wicked person is one headed for death whose inner state of wickedness requires an external warning to prompt turning — without warning, wickedness leads unimpeded to destruction.
Even when warned, the wicked person may refuse to turn from his way, showing wickedness as a persistent inner orientation that warning alone cannot overcome.
Wickedness is a way one walks in that leads to death, but God desires the wicked to turn back from it — framing wickedness as a volitional direction the inner person can reverse.
Wickedness is a relational moral state that does not doom the one who turns from it — the inner orientation toward wickedness can be abandoned and the person spared by that turning.
set.keywords: ["wickedness abandoning", "will reversing", "moral state mutable", "turning saving", "inner orientation"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked person under sentence of death can redirect inner life by turning from sin toward just action, showing wickedness as a condition the will can renounce.
The wicked person's turning is evidenced by concrete acts of restitution and lawful living — inner reversal of wickedness must express itself in outward moral behavior.
set.keywords: ["wickedness reversed", "restitution enacting", "will obeying", "inner change visible", "justice practicing"]

set.analysis_note: When the wicked turns from wickedness to just and right action, life results — affirming wickedness as a directional inner state whose reversal brings restoration.
set.keywords: ["wickedness turned", "will redirected", "justice chosen", "life restored", "inner state reversed"]

set.analysis_note: Abominations are listed alongside violence and adultery as companion corruptions — inner defiance toward God expressed through habitual moral violation.
The accumulated abominations committed by the people become the declared cause of divine desolation — inner corruption externalized into acts that destroy the land.
Restored memory of abominations produces self-loathing — abomination recalled through awakened conscience generates genuine inner revulsion at one's own past defilement.
The detestable things and transgressions that defiled the people will be completely removed by divine cleansing — defilement is an inner-and-outer condition that only God can fully purge.
The abominations committed defiled God's holy name itself — they constituted a profaning of the divine-human boundary, provoking consuming anger.
set.keywords: ["abomination defiling", "holiness violated", "boundary profaned", "divine anger provoked", "name desecrated"]

set.analysis_note: The abominations are attributed to a rebellious inner disposition — the house of rebellion has saturated itself with them and is commanded to stop.
Abominations here include allowing the uncircumcised in heart access to sacred space — inner spiritual uncircumcision is the root defilement compounding all other violations.
Those who committed abominations must bear their own shame — the abominations become a permanent inner weight of disgrace that bars them from sacred service.
The abomination here is an act of sacrilegious desolation — an external enactment of radical evil that brings desolating judgment, with less focus on individual inner state.
The abomination that makes desolate is a deliberate act of profaning the sacred — an extreme outer expression of inner hostility toward the divine.
The wicked act wickedly and cannot understand — wickedness here is an inner state of moral blindness that hardens under refinement rather than opening to wisdom.
The abomination that makes desolate is set up at a specific apocalyptic moment — it functions as a marker of peak evil rather than detailing individual inner-being content.
Evildoing is the city's constitutive character — it is tracked in blood, showing that inner malice has externalized into a pervasive culture of violent wrongdoing.
Consecrating themselves to the shameful idol transformed the people internally — they became detestable like the thing they loved, showing that devoted inner attachment shapes moral identity.
The evil of their deeds is the wickedness rooted in a specific place of rebellion that provokes divine hatred and expulsion — inner wickedness made visible in deeds that sever relationship.
set.keywords: ["evil deeds", "wickedness relational", "divine hatred provoked", "rebellion localized", "rejection consequent"]

set.analysis_note: Wickedness is the inward soil that produces injustice and lies — the people's self-trust and reliance on strength rather than God cultivated an inner field of moral corruption.
Iniquity located in a specific region characterizes its worship as false and futile — the evil is embedded in religious practice that is empty at its core.
Wickedness is devised on the bed at night and enacted at dawn — showing it as a premeditated inner exercise of the planning mind, empowered by the will and available means.
Wickedness is stored as treasure in the house — treating unjust gain as wealth reveals an inner value system corrupted at its root, where evil profit is prized.
set.keywords: ["wickedness treasured", "values corrupted", "gain unjust", "heart valuing wrong", "greed inner"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked person's house holds treasures of wickedness — the wicked identity is expressed in economic dishonesty, showing moral corruption lodged in practical daily choices.
Wickedness here is embodied in fraudulent scales and weights — the inner moral corruption expresses itself as deliberate deception in commerce and justice.
The prophet is made to see iniquity — evil as destructive trouble that assaults the inner person through forced witness of violence, strife and contention it produces.
The wicked surround the righteous and pervert justice — wickedness is an aggressive outer force whose inner character is expressed through domination and the suppression of moral order.
The wicked swallows up one more righteous than himself while God looks on silently — wickedness here is aggressive predation by the morally corrupt against the relatively innocent.
The wicked here is a house or power structure crushed by divine salvation — wickedness as entrenched institutional corruption that must be broken from the top.
The wicked are swept away alongside the rubble of creation in divine judgment — wickedness marks a category of persons whose inner corruption warrants total removal.
The abominations lodged in the mouth and teeth are to be divinely removed — defilement is an inner-to-outer pollution that only God's act can extract and cleanse.
The iniquity of household gods is expressed in the nonsense and lies they produce — empty inner reliance on false spiritual guidance that leaves the soul wandering and afflicted.
Abomination is committed through faithless mixed devotion — profaning the sanctuary by marrying a foreign god's daughter reveals an inner betrayal of covenant loyalty.
The wicked are distinguished from the righteous by whether they serve God — wickedness here is defined as non-service, an inner posture of non-devotion toward God.
The wicked will be reduced to ashes — their inner corruption leads to complete destruction under divine judgment, with no substance remaining.

Evil speech uttered against a person falsely is an external assault on reputation and identity, not an inner failing of the one spoken against; the inner-being of the slandered remains blameless while the slanderer's tongue produces moral harm.
set.keywords: ["slander spoken", "false accusation", "reputation attacked", "moral harm external", "conscience clear"]

set.analysis_note: Anything beyond a simple honest yes or no originates from an inner source of evil — extra verbal elaboration signals a compromised will or manipulative intent rooted in moral corruption.

The person described as evil is one whose inner character is defined by aggression and harm toward others; the disciple is called not to mirror that corruption but to absorb it through non-resistance.

Evil persons receive the same natural blessings as the good, indicating that inner moral corruption does not alter one's standing before divine providence, though it defines a person's fundamental character orientation.
Evil here is a power or domain from which the person needs divine deliverance, framing it as a force that threatens the soul from outside the person's own strength to resist alone.
A morally corrupted eye (inner perception/intent) floods the entire inner being with darkness, showing that evil operates through the faculty of moral perception to corrupt the whole person.
set.keywords: ["perception corrupt", "inner darkness", "moral sight", "whole person defiled", "faculty corrupted"]

set.analysis_note: In this verse kakia is rendered 'trouble' referring to each day's sufficient difficulty; the inner-being content here is minimal — it describes circumstantial burden rather than moral evil in the heart.

Humans are constitutionally evil — their inner nature is bent toward corruption — yet even from that corrupt nature they produce good acts toward their children, highlighting the tension between inner evil and natural affective impulse.
A diseased inner nature inevitably produces corrupted outward fruit; evil is a constitutional inner condition that cannot generate good outcomes, as nature determines output.

It is impossible for an evil inner nature to produce good fruit; moral constitution is determinative, establishing that evil is a deep structural condition of the inner person, not a surface behavior.
Evil thoughts are located explicitly in the heart, identifying the heart as the seat of morally corrupt cognition where hostile and malicious reasoning originates.
Being evil is an inner condition of the heart; corrupt speech is merely the overflow of that inner abundance, showing that moral evil flows outward from a corrupt heart-reservoir.
The evil person draws from an inner treasury of evil to bring forth corrupt words and deeds; evil is stored as an inner resource that the person actively draws upon to produce harm.
An entire generation is characterized as evil in its inner moral orientation, expressing itself through demanding signs rather than faith — their evil is manifest as faithless demanding.

Spirits more evil than the first fill a vacated inner space, intensifying the person's inner moral corruption; unoccupied inner space invites deeper evil, and the generation mirrors this increasing inner wickedness.
The evil one targets the heart where the word has been sown; lack of understanding leaves the heart vulnerable, and the agent of evil snatches away the implanted word before it can take root.
The sons of the evil one are defined by their origin and allegiance — their inner identity is shaped by their source in the evil one rather than by the kingdom of God.
At final judgment the evil are separated from the righteous, indicating that evil constitutes a settled inner-being identity distinguishable from righteousness at the deepest level.
Evil thoughts originate in the heart and lead to outward sins such as murder and slander; the heart is the generative inner organ from which moral evil flows into action.
The generation is evil in its inner moral orientation, shown by its persistent sign-seeking instead of trust; this evil is a collective inner disposition of faithless demanding.
The servant is called wicked because, having received mercy, he refused to extend it; his inner wickedness is exposed as hardness and ingratitude that contradicts the grace he received.
The eye being evil here refers to inner envy and grudging resentment at another's good fortune; the 'evil eye' is jealousy arising from a covetous inner disposition.

Both bad and good people are gathered to the banquet, indicating that evil here describes a general moral state that does not prevent being summoned; inner moral character is not the prerequisite for invitation.
Their malice (ponēria) is a calculated inner disposition of hostile intent — Jesus perceives it directly, revealing it as a deliberate inner movement of the will aimed at entrapment.

The abomination of desolation is an act of profound desecration standing in the holy place; the inner-being content is minimal — this is an objective event of sacrilege rather than a description of inner moral state.

The servant is wicked because he was slothful — his inner evil manifested as passive refusal to act on what he knew; wickedness here is an inner disposition of fearful inaction and faithless disengagement.
Wickedness (ponēria) is listed among inner vices — coveting, deceit, envy — emerging from the heart; it names a settled inner disposition of active malice operating alongside other corrupt drives.
Evil (ponēros) appears in a catalog of inner vices alongside envy and slander, identifying it as an inner quality of character — a constituent of the heart's corrupt inventory.

All evil things come from within the person, identifying the inner being as the sole generative source of defilement; evil is not external contamination but internal production that defiles through outward expression.

The abomination of desolation standing where it ought not to be is an objective act of desecration; inner-being content is minimal here — the term marks a sacrilegious event rather than an inner moral disposition.
Herod's evil things are a pattern of immoral acts rooted in a habitual inner disposition of lawless self-indulgence; his evil is not a single act but a characterological pattern reproved publicly.

Others label the disciple's name as evil — a false social-moral judgment imposed from outside; the inner-being of the one so labeled is not actually evil, but is wrongly stigmatized for righteous allegiance.

God is kind even to the evil — those whose inner disposition is morally corrupt and ungrateful; their evil is a settled inner character of hostility and ingratitude that does not prevent divine generosity toward them.
The evil person's heart is an inner treasury from which evil is produced; speech is the outward evidence of the inner store of corruption, making the heart the generative organ of evil expression.

Humans are constitutionally described as evil, yet even from that nature they produce acts of parental love; the contrast highlights that human inner evil is a baseline condition, not an absolute barrier to every good impulse.
set.keywords: ["constitutional evil", "nature baseline", "affection remaining", "good impulse partial", "inner bent"]

set.analysis_note: This generation is declared evil in its inner moral orientation because it demands a sign rather than trusting; their evil is a collective disposition of faithless sign-seeking that refuses trust.
set.keywords: ["generation evil", "faithless seeking", "trust refusing", "inner disposition collective", "sign demanding"]

set.analysis_note: Wickedness (ponēria) fills the inner person even while the outward religious appearance is clean; the cup's inside overflows with greed and wickedness, naming the inner being as the true location of moral corruption.

What humans exalt as admirable is an abomination in God's sight; the heart's self-justifying pride produces what is detestable before God, showing that inner self-elevation is the root of this abomination.
set.keywords: ["self justifying", "pride abominable", "heart self exalting", "divine perception", "inner detestable"]

set.analysis_note: The servant is condemned as wicked by his own stated knowledge — he knew the truth and still refused to act faithfully; his wickedness is an inner combination of knowing and willful non-compliance.

People loved darkness because their works were evil; their inner moral condition of evil produced a corresponding love for darkness over light — the will chose moral concealment because it aligned with inner corruption.
set.keywords: ["will choosing darkness", "love corrupt", "works evil", "light avoided", "inner alignment"]

set.analysis_note: The world hates the one who testifies that its works are evil; the world's inner moral condition of evil generates hatred when exposed by truthful testimony — evil resists its own disclosure.

The evil one is an external malevolent power from whose domain disciples need divine protection; inner-being content here is minimal — the verse addresses protective custody rather than an internal moral state.

The accusers define the arrested man as an evildoer to justify handing him over; the charge of wrongdoing is a social-legal accusation, and the inner-being content is their malicious false judgment rather than the accused's actual state.
Wickedness is something a person can be turned away from through divine blessing; it is a settled inner orientation of moral corruption from which repentance and transformation are possible.
Wickedness is located as an intent of the heart — a deliberate inner disposition that requires repentance and prayer for forgiveness; the heart's intent is the seat of this moral evil.
Wicked men of the rabble are those whose inner character is disposed toward mob violence and social disorder; their evil manifests as willingness to be instruments of jealous aggression.
Evil (ponēros) here refers to an objectively assessable vicious crime in a legal context; the inner-being content is minimal — it names an actionable moral offense rather than an inner disposition.
set.keywords: ["offense actionable", "crime vicious", "moral violation objective", "wrongdoing assessed", "legal evil"]

set.analysis_note: Wrong (atopos) here refers to an objectively verifiable legal wrongdoing; the inner-being dimension is minimal — it functions as a legal category for chargeable misconduct rather than naming an inner moral state.
The accusers could produce no evidence of the evil crimes Festus had expected; evil here is a category of serious moral-legal offense, and its absence in the charges reveals the accusation as hollow.

No evil has been spoken about the man by any report or letter; evil speech here is false harmful report — its absence testifies to the person's unstained reputation and lack of morally damaging accusation.
set.keywords: ["evil speech absent", "reputation intact", "slander withheld", "report clean", "accusation none"]

set.analysis_note: Unrighteousness here is an active inner operation — suppressing the truth one knows — which draws God's wrath, revealing it as a willful interior distortion of moral reality.
Unrighteousness is one element in a total inner saturation — the person is filled with it alongside envy, malice, deceit — showing the soul completely occupied by corrupt orientations.
Evil (kakia) here is listed as part of a cluster of interior vices filling the person — alongside unrighteousness and malice — indicating a disposition of settled inner badness.
Evil (ponēria) appears as one of multiple vices filling the person — specifically paired with unrighteousness — indicating a deep-seated inner wickedness saturating the whole character.
Unrighteousness here is an object of wilful obedience — the self-seeking person actively chooses it over truth — showing it as the will's preferred allegiance, inviting divine wrath.
Blasphemy is triggered not by direct speech but by the moral failure of those who claim to represent God — their conduct causes outsiders to dishonour the divine name.
Human unrighteousness is presented as a foil that paradoxically highlights God's righteousness — yet the verse resists using this contrast to excuse wrongdoing or question divine justice.
Blasphemy here is the false attribution of a morally bankrupt teaching to someone — the slander that good ends justify evil means — and Paul affirms that such misrepresentation is justly condemned.
Unrighteousness is presented as what bodily members become instruments for when submitted to sin — the body becomes the channel through which inner corruption acts outward.
The verse raises and immediately rejects the charge of injustice against God — affirming that divine sovereign choice does not constitute unrighteousness, distinguishing human and divine standards.
Evil here is something the inner person must abhor — the command calls for a deep moral revulsion seated in genuine love, so the will and affections actively oppose whatever is evil.
Blasphemy here describes the social result of misusing legitimate freedom — when good is spoken of as evil, others' consciences are damaged and God's gift is defamed.
Blasphemy (slander) is something the apostle receives but answers with entreaty — the inner response is non-retaliatory, though the slander reduces the slandered to the status of refuse.
Malice (kakia) is pictured as old leaven that corrupts the whole — an inner disposition that spreads through the person and community, contrasted with the sincerity that truth produces.
Evil (ponēria) is paired with malice as an old corrupting leaven — together they represent the inner moral state that must be purged so that authentic worship and sincerity can exist.
The evil person is one whose inner wickedness has persisted to the point that the community must expel them — the evil is now characterological, defining the person as unfit for fellowship.
Blasphemy here is the act of denouncing someone for exercising a freedom performed with thanksgiving — the inner attitude of gratitude is attacked by others' negative speech.
Love refuses to take pleasure in unrighteousness — wrongdoing is here something that could draw inner delight, but love's orientation actively refuses that satisfaction and aligns with truth instead.

Evil (kakia) is something one should remain an infant in — the command is for moral inexperience in evil to coexist with mature thinking, showing evil as a domain the inner person should never master.
Evil here refers to deeds done in the body that will be evaluated at the judgment seat — every action, whether good or evil, reflects and will reveal the inner orientation that produced it.
set.keywords: ["deeds evaluated", "accountability eschatological", "body acting", "inner orientation revealed", "judgment facing"]

set.analysis_note: The present evil age is an encompassing moral-spiritual environment whose character is evil — Christ's self-giving rescues from its domain, implying a systemic wickedness that captivates the soul.
Malice (kakia) is the last in a cluster of inner dispositions — bitterness, wrath, anger, slander — that must all be consciously put away, showing malice as a deep-seated hostile inner residue.
set.keywords: ["malice residing", "inner putting away", "hostility deep", "disposition clearing", "will renouncing"]

set.analysis_note: The days are characterized as evil — an external environment of moral corruption — which demands inner urgency and wisdom in how time is used, pressing the will toward purposeful action.

Evil here describes the spiritual forces arrayed against believers — a supra-human wickedness in the heavenly realm — making clear that the inner struggle involves opposition far beyond flesh and blood.
The evil day is a time of intensified spiritual assault when evil's power peaks — the believer's inner strength and God's armor enable them to withstand and stand firm through it.
The evil one launches flaming darts — attacks designed to penetrate and ignite the inner person — faith is the shield that intercepts these, protecting the soul from being set ablaze.
Evil deeds flow from a mind that is hostile and alienated — the inner faculty of the mind is the seat of the enmity that then produces evil conduct, showing cognition corrupting behaviour.

Malice (kakia) is one of several ingrained inner dispositions — alongside anger, wrath, slander — that must be actively stripped off, indicating a settled hostile orientation requiring wilful renunciation.
Every form of evil is to be abstained from — the command is comprehensive, calling the will to maintain vigilant avoidance across all the varied expressions that evil can take.
set.keywords: ["avoidance total", "will vigilant", "evil every form", "abstinence commanded", "moral boundary"]

set.analysis_note: Wicked and evil men are characterised by faithlessness — their wickedness is not incidental but rooted in the absence of faith, showing that trust deficiency grounds moral depravity.
The evil one is an external adversarial power from whom the Lord guards his people — divine faithfulness is the protection against an evil that would otherwise overwhelm the inner person.
set.keywords: ["evil one external", "divine guarding", "soul protected", "faithfulness defending", "adversary opposed"]

set.analysis_note: Evil suspicions arise from a mind inflated with conceit and devoid of understanding — they are an inner fruit of intellectual pride and unhealthy cravings, corrupting relationships and community.
Evil people are characterized by progressive moral deterioration — they move from bad to worse, both deceiving and self-deceived, showing evil as a self-reinforcing inner trajectory.
Every evil deed is something the Lord rescues from — evil here is framed as an active threat to the person's final wellbeing, requiring divine intervention to prevent its inner and eternal damage.
Those called abominable profess knowledge of God with their lips while their works deny him — the abomination is the inner disconnect between claimed knowledge and lived obedience.
Malice (kakia) is listed among multiple vices that once characterized the believers' former inner life — passing days in malice shows it as a habitual inner orientation, not a single act.
set.keywords: ["malice habitual", "former orientation", "inner life corrupt", "envy accompanying", "disposition former"]

set.analysis_note: An evil heart is here an unbelieving heart that leads to apostasy — unbelief and evil are fused in the heart's inner condition, with the will drifting away from the living God.

Iniquities are offences covered by divine mercy that chooses not to remember them — the verse focuses on God's gracious cancellation of the moral debt, not on the inner character of the wrongdoing itself.
An evil conscience is a defiled inner faculty that makes approach to God impossible — sprinkling clean denotes its removal so that the conscience is no longer burdened by guilt and shame.
Malice (kakia) is rampant wickedness that must be stripped away before the implanted word can take root — it is an inner contamination that blocks receptivity and salvation.
Evil thoughts are the inner source of discriminatory judgment — when the mind generates such thoughts it becomes corrupt, passing unjust verdicts based on partiality rather than truth.
Boasting born of arrogance is declared evil — the inner pride that produces self-aggrandizing speech is itself the wickedness, showing how will and tongue express corrupt self-elevation.
Malice heads a list of inner dispositions — deceit, hypocrisy, envy, slander — that must all be put away, showing it as the root hostile orientation underlying the others.
set.keywords: ["malice rooting", "disposition putting away", "inner clearing", "hostility underlying", "will renouncing"]

set.analysis_note: Wrongdoing is what opponents falsely accuse believers of — the irony is that honorable conduct will eventually silence the accusation, as good deeds speak louder than slander.

Wrongdoing is the behaviour civil authority is tasked to punish — those who do evil attract external corrective judgment, while right conduct receives praise, showing evil as socially destructive action.
Evil here is the corrupt aim that freedom can be misused to serve — when liberty becomes a cover for wickedness, the inner person exploits good gifts to conceal corrupt motives.
Wrongdoing is what slanderers falsely charge believers with — a good conscience and honorable behaviour expose the false accusation, turning shame back on those who make it.
set.keywords: ["wrongdoing falsely charged", "conscience good", "slander exposed", "honor vindicating", "shame reversed"]

set.analysis_note: Wrongdoing encompasses serious harmful acts — murder, theft, meddling — that would justify suffering; believers are to ensure any suffering they bear is not deserved punishment for such acts.
The evil one is a personal spiritual adversary whom young men have overcome — spiritual strength and victory over this external evil force is presented as an inner achievement of faith and endurance.
set.keywords: ["evil one overcome", "adversary personal", "victory inner", "strength spiritual", "faith conquering"]

set.analysis_note: Overcoming the evil one is credited to inner strength sustained by God's word — the word abiding in the person is what enables the will to overcome the adversary's power.
set.keywords: ["word abiding", "evil one overcome", "strength inner", "will sustained", "adversary defeated"]

set.analysis_note: The murderous act arose from an inner moral disposition — deeds that were evil in contrast to a brother's righteousness, showing that wickedness rooted in the will drives violent hostility toward the good.
set.keywords: ["will corrupted", "moral envy", "hostility toward righteous", "deeds evil", "inner disposition"]

set.analysis_note: The evil one is portrayed as an external corrupting power that cannot penetrate the one born of God, implying that the inner person protected by divine birth is shielded from evil's touch.
The whole world lies under the dominion of the evil one, meaning humanity's collective inner orientation is captive to an evil governing power apart from God.
Associating with one who does wicked works makes one a participant in that wickedness, showing that inner complicity is communicated through voluntary relational association.
Wicked speech — malicious, slanderous nonsense — combined with refusal to show hospitality and active obstruction of others reveals an inner posture of domineering malice against community.
Dreamers defile flesh, reject authority, and blaspheme glorious ones — showing that blasphemy here arises from a wilful inner rejection of legitimate authority combined with sensory self-indulgence.

Blasphemy is directed at what is not understood, revealing that the inner faculty of rational comprehension is bypassed — contempt fills the void of ignorance, and instinct without understanding destroys.

The beast's mouth becomes the instrument of sustained, deliberate blasphemy targeting God's name, his dwelling, and heaven's inhabitants — a total inner orientation of defiant contempt toward the divine.
Despite being scorched by divine judgment, people curse God's name and refuse to repent — revealing a will hardened against God even under direct punitive pressure, with glory withheld.
In pain and suffering people curse God rather than repent of their deeds, showing that affliction deepens the hardened will's hostility rather than breaking it toward repentance.
Catastrophic hail drives people to curse God rather than seek him, showing that the will, fully hardened, interprets devastating judgment as cause for defiant blasphemy rather than contrition.
The golden cup full of abominations and sexual impurities images the inner corruption concealed within outward splendour — moral filth held and offered from within an attractive exterior.
The forehead name identifies the figure as the source and mother of all earthly abominations, imaging an identity constituted entirely by the generation and propagation of moral corruption.
Entry into the holy city is barred to those who practice abomination or falsehood, showing that such inner dispositions are fundamentally incompatible with the pure divine presence.
